p_question-3132.ja: 以下は、小説家が書いたエッセイである。 十人十色 マニュアルというものが、この世には存在する。機械を買った場合には、これを読む。 書かれてある通りに動かないと困る。ビデオの再生ボタンを押したのに、録画が始まって はたまらない。ところが、生き物はそうはいかない。あちらに通用したことが、 [41] 。 うちで、ねこを飼い始めた当座は、何も分からなかった。吐いたりすると、それだけで びっくりしてしまった。あわてて、ねこを飼っている人に電話した。一番にかけたところ が留守だと、ますます、動揺する。結局、関西の知り合いにまでかけて、「心配ありませんよ。ねこは吐くものですよ」 という言葉をいただき、やっと安心。こんな具合だった。 さて、 [42] 時に、当然のことながら「ねこの飼い方」の本も読んだ。マニュアルである。なるほど——と思えることが書いてある。中でも納得したのが、 [43] 。 ——「動物にとって、用足ししている注１時は、最も無防備な状態注２です。襲われたら大ピンチ。その最中、人に近づかれることを、ねこはとても嫌います。飼い主は、離れるようにし、のびのびとした気分でさせてやりましょう」 これは頷うなずける。そこで、ゆずが うちのねこの名前はゆずという そうする時は遠 慮していた。 [44] 。朝、ねこトイレ注３の砂をかきまわし、汚れ物を取り始めると、「ご苦労」というように、ゆずがやって来る。そして、まだトイレに手を入れているのに、「どけどけ」というように中に入ってくる。そして、足を踏ん張り、——行うのだ。これ見よがしに注４。 あの説得力のあるマニュアルは、一体全体、何だったのか。なるほど、生きている物には個性があると、あらためて [45] 。 （北村薫『書かずにはいられない 北村薫のエッセイ』新潮社による） （注１）用足ししている:大便や小便をしている （注２）無防備な状態:危険に備えていない様子 （注３）ねこトイレ:箱の底に砂などを敷いた、ねこ用のトイレ （注４）これ見よがしに:自慢げに見せつけるように
p_question-3132.en: The following is an essay written by a novelist. Different Strokes for Different Folks Manuals exist in this world. When you buy a machine, you read them. If it doesn’t operate as written, you’re in trouble. You wouldn’t want recording to start when you press the play button on a video player. Living creatures, however, aren’t like that. What worked for one will be [41] . When we first started keeping a cat at our place, we knew nothing. If it vomited, that alone startled us. In a panic, I phoned people who kept cats. If the first person I called was out, I got even more agitated. I ended up calling an acquaintance in Kansai, and they said, “Don’t worry. Cats vomit.” Hearing those words finally put me at ease. It was like that. Then, [42] of course I also read books on “How to Keep a Cat.” A manual. They contained things that made me think, “I see.” What I found most convincing was [43] . — “For animals, the time when they are relieving themselvesNote 1 is when they are in their most defenseless stateNote 2. If attacked then, it’s a huge pinch. Cats dislike people approaching them in the middle of that. Owners should keep their distance and let them do it in a relaxed mood.” This made sense. So when Yuzu—our cat’s name is Yuzu—was doing that, I kept my distance. [44] . In the morning, when I stir the sand in the cat toiletNote 3 and start removing the soiled clumps, Yuzu comes over as if to say, “Thanks for your trouble.” And even while my hand is still in the litter box, he pushes in as if to say, “Move, move,” braces his legs, and—does his business. OstentatiouslyNote 4. What on earth was that persuasive manual? Indeed, I was reminded anew that living things each have their individuality, [45] . (From Kaoru Kitamura, “I Can’t Help but Write: Essays by Kaoru Kitamura,” Shinchosha) (Note 1) Relieving themselves: defecating or urinating (Note 2) Defenseless state: being unprepared for danger (Note 3) Cat toilet: a cat litter box with sand etc. spread on the bottom (Note 4) Ostentatiously: in a showy, flaunting manner

p_question-3137.ja: (1) 教師＝話す人、生徒＝聞く人という構造が知らず知らずのうちに教室空間にできあがり、 そして固定化してしまうのは恐ろしいことではないかと思う。教師が先取りしてしまうこ とで、生徒が自分自身で考え、解決しようとする芽をつみとってしまう場合がある。 いつも話し続けるのがコミュニケーションでない。教師側が沈黙し、「待つ」という行 為も時には大切であろう。もう少し話したい、と思うところで 一歩ひいてみる注ことで、相 手が言おうとすることを引き出すことができるのである。 （徳井厚子『日本語教師の「衣」再考 多文化共生への課題』くろしお出版による） （注）一歩ひいてみる:ここでは、話すのをやめてみる
p_question-3137.en: (1) I think it’s frightening when, without anyone noticing, a structure of “teacher = speaker, students = listeners” takes shape in the classroom and then becomes fixed. When the teacher preempts everything, it can nip in the bud the students’ attempts to think for themselves and try to solve things on their own. Communication is not about talking all the time. At times it is important for the teacher to be silent and to “wait.” By taking a step backNote at the very point where you feel like saying a little more, you can draw out what the other person is trying to say. (Tokui Atsuko, “Rethinking the ‘Attire’ of Japanese Language Teachers: Issues Toward Multicultural Cohabitation,” Kurosio Publishing) (Note) Taking a step back: here, to stop speaking for a moment

p_question-3138.ja: (2) 以下は、ある市役所のホームページに掲載されたお知らせである。 2016年11月1日 スポーツ課 市民運動場の予約について 市民運動場の予約は、これまで管理事務所窓口で受け付けておりましたが、2017 年2月1日よりインターネット上の予約システムでも行うことができるようにな ります。予約システムの利用は平日、土日祝日を問わず24時間可能で、予約は、 窓口での予約と同様に、使用日の一か月前から受け付けます。 予約システムの利用に際しては、事前に利用者登録が必要となりますので、身分 を証明できるものを持って管理事務所窓口にお越しください。 市民運動場管理事務所 〒002-3833 南松市中央町3-2 中央公園内 （受付時間:月曜日～金曜日 9:00～17:00）
p_question-3138.en: (2) The following is a notice posted on a city hall website. November 1, 2016 Sports Division Regarding Reservations for the Civic Sports Ground Until now, reservations for the civic sports ground were accepted at the management office counter, but from February 1, 2017, they can also be made via an online reservation system. The reservation system is available 24 hours a day on weekdays, weekends, and holidays, and reservations are accepted starting one month before the date of use, the same as for counter reservations. To use the reservation system, you must register as a user in advance. Please come to the management office counter with identification. Civic Sports Ground Management Office 3-2 Chuo-cho, Minamimatsu City 002-3833 (inside Chuo Park) (Office hours: Monday–Friday, 9:00–17:00)

p_question-3139.ja: (3) 異文化間での対話を議論するときに，必ずといってよいくらい出てくるのが，価値観の 理解と共有である。他者と対話を通して，人間関係を樹立していくには，自己の価値観を 保存したままで，他者の価値観を理解するという方略だけでは十分ではない。相互的な働 きかけを通じて，何か新たな価値を共有することが要求されるのである。すなわち，自ら の価値観を相対化し，新たな価値を対話という共同作業を通して創り上げ，それを共有し ていく態度が必要なのだ。 （ARCLE編集委員会・田中茂範・アレン玉井光江・根岸雅史・吉田研作編著 『幼児から成人まで一貫した英語教育のための枠組み ECF EnglishCurriculumFramework』 リーベル出版による）
p_question-3139.en: (3) When discussing dialogue across cultures, almost without fail the topic that arises is understanding and sharing values. To build relationships with others through dialogue, it is not enough to keep one’s own values intact while merely understanding the other’s values. What is required is to share some new value through mutual engagement. In other words, we need an attitude that relativizes our own values, creates new values through the joint work called dialogue, and shares them. (ARCLE Editorial Committee / Shigenori Tanaka / Mitsue Allen-Tamai / Masashi Negishi / Kensaku Yoshida, eds.,“A Framework for Continuous English Education from Early Childhood to Adulthood: ECF English Curriculum Framework,”Liberal Publishing)

p_question-3141.ja: (4) 以下は、劇を作ることを仕事にしている人が書いた文章である。 僕は「変な人」です。そうでなければ、こんな仕事はしてません。そして僕は「普通の人」です。だからこそこの仕事が成立しています。 「特別なもの」を生み出そうとするとき、それがどんなふうに特別なのかを「普通」という視点から見極める必要があります。「特別」と「普通」、定規を何度も持ち替えるのです。そのために自分の中の普通さを死守するのです。 （小林賢太郎『僕がコントや演劇のために考えていること』幻冬舎による）
p_question-3141.en: (4) The following was written by someone whose job is creating plays. I am a “strange person.” Otherwise I wouldn’t be doing this kind of work. And I am a “normal person.” That is precisely why this work functions. When trying to create something “special,” you need to determine in what way it is special from the perspective of “ordinary.” You keep switching rulers between “special” and “ordinary.” For that, you must defend the ordinariness within yourself to the last. (Kentarō Kobayashi, “What I Think About for Sketch Comedy and Theater,” Gentosha)

p_question-3145.ja: (1) 人に従順な飼い犬は、もともとオオカミの仲間を飼い馴ならしたものである。（中略） ところが、「人間がオオカミを飼い馴ならした」という話には謎が多い。犬が人間と暮らすようになったのは、15000年ほど前の旧石器時代のことであると推測されている。当時の人類にとって、肉食獣にくしょくじゅうは恐るべき敵であった。そんな恐ろしい肉食獣にくしょくじゅうを飼い馴ならすという発想を当時の人類が持ち得たのだろうか。しかも犬を飼うということは、犬にエサをやらなければならない。わずかな食糧で暮らしていた人類に、犬を飼うほどの余裕があったのだろうか。また当時の人類は犬がいなくても、狩りをすることができた。犬を必要とする理由はなかったのである。 最近の研究では、人間が犬を必要としたのではなく、犬の方から人間を求めて寄り添っ てきたと考えられている。犬の祖先となったとされる弱いオオカミたちは、群れの中での 順位が低く、食べ物も十分ではない。そこで、人間に近づき、食べ残しをあさるように なったのではないかと考えられているのである。 弱いオオカミだけでは、狩りをすることができないが、人間の手助けをすることはでき る。そして、やがて人間と犬とが共に狩りをするようになったと推察されている。こう考 えると、当時、自然界の中で強い存在となりつつあった人間に寄り添うことは、犬にとっ て得なことが多かった。つまり、人間が犬を利用したのではなく、犬が人間を利用したか もしれないのである。 （稲垣栄洋『弱者の戦略』新潮社による）
p_question-3145.en: (1) The obedient pet dog originally comes from taming members of the wolf family. (Omitted) However, the story that “humans tamed wolves” contains many mysteries. It is estimated that dogs began living with humans in the Paleolithic, about 15,000 years ago. For humans of that time, carnivores were terrifying enemies. Could people back then really have had the idea of taming such fearsome carnivores? Moreover, keeping dogs means feeding them. Did humans, who lived on meager food supplies, have the leeway to keep dogs? Also, people of that time could hunt even without dogs. There was no reason they needed dogs. Recent studies suggest that it was not humans who needed dogs, but dogs that sought out humans and came to stay close. The weak wolves thought to be the ancestors of dogs ranked low within their packs and did not have enough food. It is therefore thought that they approached humans and began scavenging leftovers. Weak wolves by themselves could not hunt, but they could assist humans. Eventually, it is inferred, humans and dogs began to hunt together. Seen this way, for dogs it brought many benefits to stay close to humans, who were becoming a powerful presence in nature at that time. In other words, perhaps it was not that humans used dogs, but that dogs used humans. (Inagaki Yoshihiro, “Strategy of the Weak,” Shinchosha)

p_question-3149.ja: (2) 子どもはこれから自分は大人になっていくのだから、自分はどうなるのだろうとそれは 一所懸命に大人を観察している。その大人に魅力を感じれば、あんなふうになりたいと思 うかもしれない。ほんのちょっとチャーミングなところを認めて、ああ失敗しても、 どじ注１ばかりでもいいんだと思えることもあるかもしれない。あるいは、僕はあんな大人にはな らないだろうけれど、あんなふうにするのもすてきだなと感じることもあるに違いない。 とにかく子どもは、 そんなふうに①常に大人を見ているのである。 （中略） 子どもはやがて大人になる。その大人に魅力がなかったら、それは自分に明日がないと 言われているのと同じことだ。大人になってもつまらなそうだ、楽しいことがなさそうだ と感じたら、君の未来はこの程度のものだとつきつけられているのと変わらない。 これほど子どもにとって不幸なことはない②。 大人はいつも子どもに見つめられている、子どもが自分を観察しているということを自 覚していなければいけないと思う。わが身をつくろって、いいかっこするのではない。正 直に失敗するのなら、子どもより上手に失敗してみせよう、傷つくなら子どもより上手に 傷ついてみせよう。人生の先輩としてというより、現役の子どもに対してベテランの子ど もとして、ベテランらしいところを見せてやろうじゃないか。そういう気概きがいの注２大人がたくさんいれば、子どもたちはきっと大人の世界に魅力を見いだすに違いない。それが幸福な 子どもの将来につながるのだと思う。 （大林宣彦『父の失恋 娘の結婚 べそっかきの幸福そうな顔』フレーベル館による） （注１）どじ:うっかりした失敗 き がい （注２）気概きがいの:ここでは、強い気持ちを持った
p_question-3149.en: (2) Children, knowing that they themselves will become adults, watch adults intently, wondering what they will turn into. If they find an adult appealing, they may think they want to be like that. Noticing just a hint of charm, they might feel, “Ah, even if I fail or am a bit of a klutz, that’s okay.” Or they may think, “I won’t become that kind of adult, but doing it like that is wonderful.” In any case, children are always watching adults in that way①. (Omitted) Children will one day become adults. If those adults have no appeal, it’s the same as being told that the child has no tomorrow. If adulthood looks boring and joyless, it’s no different from having it thrust at them that “your future amounts to this.” Nothing could be more unfortunate for a child②. Adults must remain aware that children are always watching them, observing them. It’s not about patching yourself up and trying to look good. If we are going to fail, let’s fail more skillfully than children do; if we are going to be hurt, let’s show how to be hurt more skillfully than children. Not so much as life’s seniors, but as veteran children toward children currently in the game, let’s show what a veteran looks like. If there are many adults with that kind of resolve(Note 2), children will surely find the adult world attractive. I believe that leads to a happy future for children. (Nobuhiko Obayashi, “Father’s Heartbreak, Daughter’s Marriage: The Tearful Yet Happy Face,” Froebel-kan) (Note 1) doji: a careless mistake (Note 2) with resolve: here, having strong determination

p_question-3152.ja: (3) 科学記者を始めた２ ０年ほど前、記者の訪問を歓迎しない科学者は、けっして珍しくな かった。「新聞記者との付き合いには何のメリットもなく、時間の無駄。記者と親しい科 学者は、同僚からうさんくさい目で見られる。真理の探究に没頭する科学者が、記者なんていう世俗を相手にしては 沽券こけんにかかわる注1」というわけだ。それが今は、まったく違う。科学者も、研究に税金を使うからには自分の仕事を積極的に世間に説明するのが当然だと みなされ、大学や研究所はメディア戦略を練るまでになった。変われば変わるものだ。 （中略） 科学者側の広報が巧みになればなるほど、科学ジャーナリズムは科学者集団のたんなる宣 伝係で仕事をした気になってしまう恐れがある。 「サイエンス」や英国の「ネイチャー」に載る科学者の論文を、どの新聞も毎週のよう に記事にして紹介している。その多くが、これらの論文誌の巧みな広報資料や研究者の記 者発表をもとにしているのだが、これなどまさに、何を社会に伝えるかは自分で決めるというジャーナリズムの 要かなめ注２を、科学者集団側になかば預けてしまっているのではないか。 自分でネタ探しをするよりも、このほうがたしかに効率的なのだ。 米国の科学ジャーナリズムの教科書には、科学者たちはマスメディアを自分たちの広報 機関のようにとらえるものだと書いてある。科学ジャーナリズムは、広報戦略に長たけてきた注３科学者たちとどう付き合っていくべきか。その哲学と戦略を、こちら側も改あらためて肝きもに銘めいじて注４おかなければならない時代になった。 （YOMIURI ONLINE ＜http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/column/science/20090513-OYT8T00319.htm＞2010年3月7日取得による） (注1) 沽券こけんにかかわる：体面を損ねる (注2) 要かなめ：最も大切な部分 (注3) 長たけてきた：上手になってきた (注4) 肝きもに銘めいじて：忘れないように心にしっかりととどめて
p_question-3152.en: (3) When I started as a science reporter about twenty years ago, it was by no means rare to find scientists who did not welcome visits from reporters. Their view was, “There’s no benefit to dealing with newspaper reporters; it’s a waste of time. Scientists who are friendly with reporters are looked at suspiciously by colleagues. A scientist absorbed in the pursuit of truth would damage their reputation(Note 1) by engaging with something as worldly as reporters.” Now, things are completely different. Since research uses tax money, scientists are considered to have a duty to explain their work proactively to the public, and universities and research institutes have gone so far as to devise media strategies. How times change. (Omitted) The more skillful the scientists’ public relations become, the greater the risk that science journalism will feel it has done its job while serving merely as the scientists’ publicity arm. Newspapers make weekly articles out of scientists’ papers published in “Science” and the UK’s “Nature.” Many of these pieces are based on those journals’ skillful press materials and researchers’ press briefings. In doing so, aren’t we in effect entrusting, at least in part, journalism’s very keystoneNote 2—the decision of what to convey to society—to the community of scientists? It is certainly more efficient than digging up stories on one’s own. Textbooks on U.S. science journalism say that scientists tend to regard mass media as their own PR apparatus. How should science journalism deal with scientists who have become adeptNote 3 at public-relations strategies? It has become a time when we on this side must engrave on our heartsNote 4 our own philosophy and strategy for that relationship. (YOMIURI ONLINE 「http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/column/science/20090513-OYT8T00319.htm」 Retrieved March 7, 2010) (Note 1) damage one’s reputation: to lose face (Note 2) keystone: the most important part (Note 3) adept: have become skilled (Note 4) engrave on one’s heart: to remember firmly and not forget

p_question-3155.ja: 暮らしの中で身近な木といえば、街路樹と公園の樹木、そして住宅の庭の木あたりで しょうか。いずれも毎日目にはしているものの、あらためて意識することは少ないと思い ます。でも、例えばこれがすべて枯れてしまったとしたらどうでしょう。なんとも寂しく、 無味乾燥な、あるいは何か病気を連想させるようなイメージのまちになってしまうのでは ないでしょうか。また、昨今は、維持管理の面などから街路樹を植えないまちなどもある ようですが、一見近代的、未来都市的なイメージもしますが、うるおいややすらぎのない まちのようにも見えます。このようにまちの樹木は、実はとても大きな役割を持っていま す。 では、この木々たちは、ただ植えるだけ、存在するだけでいいのでしょうか。そうでは ありません。そこに意味や意義がなければならないのです。わかりやすく言うと、街路樹 じゅしゅ の樹種を何にするかというようなことです。 その土地の植生注１を踏まえ、その上に歴史性や未来性を重ね合わせる。季節の移ろいの中で、人々がその木をどのように眺めながら暮ら していくのか。そんな積み重ねの上にはじめて「ここにはこの木を植えよう」ということ になる。 それ①がその木がその場所に存在する意義です。 住宅の庭木も同じです。単に自分の好みばかりでなく、その木が住宅街の小路をどのよ うに演出するのか、まわりとの調和はどうなのか。そんなことを考えていくのがまちづく りの中の「木」です。昨今のガーデニングブームで、確かに個々の家の庭は立派になりま した。花や木の種類もずいぶん増えて、ひと昔前には無かったような色や形も見られます。 そして、ガーデニングをする人達の情報交流も盛んとなり、新たなコミュニティも生まれ ているようです。しかし、いま一つ自分の土地から外に広がっていない感じがします。道 路や公園は地域にとっての共有の庭であり、個々の部分と共有の部分が美しくなってこそ はじめて全体が美しくなるのです。美しく楽しい庭を作っている人々には、 もっと欲張って②美しく楽しいまちを作ってほしいと思います。 「愛めでる」という言葉があります。これは主に植物に対して使われます。満開の桜や初 夏の新緑、真夏の木陰や秋の紅葉・・・。私たちは 折々に注２木々を眺め、そこに日々の暮らしを重ね合わせたり、育ちゆく木々に子供達の明るい未来を願ったりしているのではないでしょうか。そしてそんな思いをこめて水やりや手入れをする。これが「愛めでる」ということだと思うのです。その愛めでる心と愛めでられる木々があってはじめてよいまちとなるの です。 （加藤美浩『まちづくりのススメ』による） （注１）その土地の植生:その土地にどのような植物が生えているか （注２）折々に:ここでは、機会があるごとに
p_question-3155.en: When we think of trees close to our daily lives, perhaps we mean street trees, park trees, and the trees in our home gardens. We see them every day, yet we rarely take conscious note of them. But what if, for example, all of them withered? Wouldn’t the town take on a lonely, barren image—perhaps even one that calls illness to mind? In recent years, some towns have opted not to plant street trees for reasons such as maintenance, which can give an ostensibly modern, futuristic impression, yet can also make a town look devoid of moisture and calm. In this way, urban trees in fact play a very large role. Then, is it enough merely to plant these trees and have them exist? No, it is not. There must be meaning and significance there. To put it simply, it is a question like which species to choose for street trees. Based on the local vegetationNote 1, we should layer on historical character and a sense of the future. How will people live while looking at those trees amid the changing seasons? Only after such accumulation do we arrive at “Let’s plant this tree here.” That① is the significance of that tree’s presence in that place. The same goes for trees in residential gardens. It should not be only a matter of personal taste; we should think about how a tree will help stage a residential lane and how it harmonizes with its surroundings. That is what “trees” mean within town planning. With the recent gardening boom, individual home gardens have certainly become splendid. The varieties of flowers and trees have greatly increased, and we now see colors and shapes that didn’t exist a generation ago. Information exchange among gardeners has become lively, and new communities seem to be forming. However, there is a lingering sense that this has not quite extended beyond one’s own property. Roads and parks are the neighborhood’s shared garden, and only when the individual parts and the shared parts become beautiful does the whole become beautiful. To those creating beautiful, enjoyable gardens, I would like to say: be even more ambitious② and create beautiful, enjoyable towns. There is the word “mederu,” to cherish or take delight in. It is used mainly for plants. Cherry blossoms in full bloom, the fresh green of early summer, shade in midsummer, autumn leaves… We from time to timeNote 2 gaze at trees, overlaying them with our daily lives, and in growing trees we may wish for our children’s bright future. With those feelings we water and tend them. I believe this is what it means to “cherish.” Only with hearts that cherish and trees that are cherished can we have a good town. (Kato Yoshihiro, “A Guide to Town-Making”) (Note 1) the local vegetation: what kinds of plants grow in that area (Note 2) from time to time: here, on each suitable occasion

p_question-3160.ja: Ａ 雑談はいろいろな意見を交換し合うことによって、ヒントを得ようというスケールの 大きな場である。そこにいる誰もが自由に発言する権利を持っている。仮に自分とは反 対意見であっても、まずは聞くという姿勢を保つこと、心理学のカウンセリングと同じ である。 そして相手の発言に対して、自分の意見を軽い気分で述べる、それが雑談である。 どんなに間違っている、バカバカしいと思われる意見であっても、いったんそれを受 け入れること。「なぜあの人はこのような発言をするのか」と考えていくと、自分がそ れまで見落としていたことがあることに気がつくこともある。 「話し上手は聞き上手」という言葉があるように、雑談では「いかに発言するか」より も「いかに聞くか」が大切になる。 （多湖輝『人の心をつかむ「雑談力」情報が集まる「雑談力」 』新講社による） Ｂ 雑談は無駄だという人がいるが、本当にそうだろうか。辞書を調べると「無駄話」と いう意味もあるが、「さまざまなことを気楽に話し合うこと」という意味もある。気楽 な気持ちのとき、人は本音を話すものだ。バカらしいと思う話もあるかもしれないが、 雑談の中から相手の人間性が見えてくる。 そうはいっても、気楽に話せる雰囲気を作るのは簡単なことではない。まずは、自分 から話のきっかけになりそうな小さなエピソードを話そう。相手が 話に乗って注きたと思ったら、そこで自分の本音を話してみよう。そうすれば、相手もやがて心を開いて話 し始めるだろう。そうなれば、雑談も意味のある時間となる。 （注）話に乗る:ここでは、話に興味を持つ
p_question-3160.en: A Small talk is a grand arena for exchanging various opinions in order to gain hints. Everyone present has the right to speak freely. Even if an opinion runs counter to your own, you should first maintain an attitude of listening—just as in psychological counseling. And in response to the other person’s remarks, you casually offer your own view—that’s what small talk is. No matter how wrong or ridiculous an opinion may seem, first accept it. If you think, “Why is that person making such a remark?” you may notice something you yourself had overlooked until then. As the saying goes, “A good speaker is a good listener.” In small talk, “how to listen” is more important than “how to speak.” (Togo Akira, “The Power of Small Talk that Grabs People’s Hearts; The Power of Small Talk that Attracts Information,” Shinko-sha) B Some say small talk is a waste, but is it really? A dictionary shows it can mean “idle chatter,” but it also means “talking about various things in a relaxed way.” When people feel at ease, they tend to speak their true feelings. Some talk may seem silly, but through small talk you can see the other person’s humanity. Still, creating an atmosphere where people can speak easily is not simple. First, tell a small episode of your own that could serve as a conversation starter. If you feel the other person is getting into the conversationNote, then try sharing your own true feelings. In time, the other person will likely open up and begin to talk. When that happens, small talk becomes meaningful time. (Note) “Gets into the conversation”: here, becomes interested in the talk

p_question-3164.ja: 建築の設計をやっていると様々な職人に出会う。大小を問わずどの現場でも一人や二人、 主役を張注１れる人がいる。そうした人に出会うのが、現場に通う楽しみのひとつだ。長い時間、図面にばかり接していると、現実を離れて思考が一人歩きすることがよくある。そん な時、彼らからもらう情報が かけがえのない注２ものであることが分かる。我々が作り出す図面は、線で描かれた抽象的な記号に過ぎない。彼らは物に触っている。経験則によって裏 付けられた、物に近い、深くて確かな情報を持っている。 図面は人間の頭の中だけで作り出されたものだ。それを現実の建物に移し替えるには、 木や鉄やコンクリートといった、物から手によって直接に得られる情報が不可欠だ。頭で 生み出されたものは、思いこみや錯誤によって間違うことが多いからだ。 今はコンピューターと情報通信の時代だ。それにともなって、手を動かす機会がどんど ん少なくなってきている。建築の設計でもCAD（コンピューター利用設計）化の勢いは すさまじい。しかし、その図面は、設計の全体を把握しにくい。きれい過ぎて、何であれ、 すべてうまくいっているように見えてしまう。手を経ずに、頭の中だけで作業が完結して しまっているからだろう。 トレーシングペーパー注３に鉛筆で苦労をして描かれた 旧来の注４図面は、そこに描く人の感情が入っている。うまくいっていないところは消しゴムで消し、描き直して修正していく。 技術的に問題のあるところ、デザイン的にうまくいっていないところほど、線はにじみ、 トレーシングペーパーは人の手の脂で汚れてくる。何回も描き直した個所は、しまいには 擦り切れて穴が開いてしまうこともある。 描いた当人の自信がなければ、鉛筆の線にもその迷いを見て取ることもできる。慣れて くると、図面上の線から、描いた人の経験的なレベルや人柄さえ分かるようになる。手書 きの図面には、すてがたい様々な種類の情報が塗り込められている。均質な図面の向こう 側に人の姿が見えにくい分、CADでは大きなリスクを見落とす可能性もある。 手から遠いコンピューターの出現によって、リスクの所在をかぎ取ることが、旧来の経 験則では難しくなってきている。これは設計に限ったことではないだろう。今や情報通信 とコンピューターはあらゆる分野に浸透し、社会全体を変えつつある。頭から生み出され たものが暴走している。リスクの所在が、より巨大で、見えにくくなった。 どこかでそれを、生身の身体を持つ人間の側に引き戻す必要がある。手から得られる情 報は、効率は悪いが、現実の世界を まさぐって注５得られるものだ。その人の身体だけにとどまる固有の情報といってもよい。忘れられつつある手の行き場を考えるべきだろう。 （内藤廣『建築のはじまりに向かって』王国社による） （注１）主役を張る:ここでは、主要な役割を果たす （注２）かけがえのない:他に代わりがないほど貴重な （注３）トレーシングペーパー:ここでは、設計図を描くための紙 （注４）旧来の:昔からの （注５）まさぐる:手探りをする
p_question-3164.en: In doing architectural design, you meet all sorts of craftspeople. On sites large and small alike, there are always one or two people who can take the leading roleNote 1. Meeting such people is one of the pleasures of visiting sites. When you spend long hours facing nothing but drawings, your thinking often breaks away from reality and runs off on its own. At such times you realize the information you get from them is irreplaceableNote 2. The drawings we produce are nothing more than abstract symbols rendered in lines. They, on the other hand, touch materials. They possess deep, reliable information close to the thing itself, backed by rules of thumb. Drawings are created solely in the human mind. To translate them into actual buildings requires information gained directly by hand from things—wood, steel, concrete, and so on. What is produced in the head is often wrong due to assumptions or errors. This is the age of computers and information networks. Along with that, opportunities to use our hands are rapidly decreasing. In architectural design, the momentum toward CAD is tremendous. Yet those drawings make it hard to grasp the whole of a design. They are so neat that everything looks as if it’s going well. Perhaps it’s because the work is completed only in the head, without passing through the hands. Old-styleNote 4 drawings painstakingly done in pencil on tracing paperNote 3 contain the drafter’s emotions. Where things don’t go well, you erase with an eraser and redraw to correct. The more technical problems there are, or the more the design falls short, the more the lines blur, and the tracing paper becomes stained with the oils from one’s hands. Places redrawn many times may even get worn through and end up with holes. If the person who drew it lacks confidence, you can see that hesitation in the pencil lines. With experience, from the lines on a drawing you can even discern the drafter’s experiential level and personality. Hand-drawn drawings are imbued with all sorts of invaluable information. Precisely because the human figure is harder to see beyond uniform drawings, CAD carries the possibility of overlooking major risks. With the advent of computers—distant from the hand—it has become difficult, by the old experiential rules, to sniff out where risks lie. This likely isn’t limited to design. Now information networks and computers have permeated every field and are changing society as a whole. What is produced in the head is running amok. The location of risk has become larger and harder to see. Somewhere, we need to pull this back to the side of human beings with living bodies. Information gained from the hand is inefficient, but it is obtained by gropingNote 5 through the real world. You could call it information unique to and confined within that person’s body. We ought to consider where those increasingly forgotten hands can find their place. (Naito Hiroshi, “Toward the Beginning of Architecture,” Oukoku-sha) (Note 1) take the leading role: here, play a principal role (Note 2) irreplaceable: so valuable that nothing can take its place (Note 3) tracing paper: here, paper used to draw plans (Note 4) old-style: traditional, from earlier times (Note 5) grope: to feel one’s way by hand

questions.stem: 社会活動に参加することで、人脈を広げることができた。
opts: じんみゃく
じんまく
にんみゃく
にんまく
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: じんみゃく is correct. Question Translation "社会活動に参加することで、人脈を広げることができた。" → "By participating in social activities, I was able to expand my network." Explanation The kanji "人脈" is read as じんみゃく, which means *network* or *personal connections*. This is the correct reading of the kanji in the sentence. Other Options 2. じんまく – Sounds similar but is not a reading of 人脈. 3. にんみゃく – Not a standard reading of 人脈; *脈* is typically read as みゃく. 4. にんまく – Does not match any standard reading for this usage. Only じんみゃく corresponds to the kanji 人脈 in this context.

questions.stem: 鈴木すず きさんは指摘がいつも的確で、本当に賢い人だと思う。
opts: するどい
かしこい
すごい
えらい
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: かしこい is correct. Question Translation "鈴木さんは指摘がいつも的確で、本当に賢い人だと思う。" → "I think Mr. Suzuki's observations are always precise, making him truly a wise person." Explanation The kanji "賢い" is read as かしこい, which means *wise* or *smart*. This reading is appropriate because the context describes Mr. Suzuki's precise observations, suggesting intelligence. Other Options 1. するどい – Means *sharp* or *acute* (kanji: 鋭い), does not match 賢い. 3. すごい – Means *amazing* or *great* (kanji: 凄い), unrelated to 賢い. 4. えらい – Means *great* or *distinguished* (kanji: 偉い), not the correct reading for 賢い. Only かしこい matches the kanji 賢い in this context.

questions.stem: 文化の違いが食生活に顕著に現れている。
opts: げんちょ
けんしょ
けんちょ
げんしょ
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: けんちょ is correct. Question Translation "文化の違いが食生活に顕著に現れている。" → "Cultural differences are prominently reflected in dietary habits." Explanation The kanji "顕著" is read as けんちょ, which means *prominent* or *remarkable*. This reading is correct based on the kanji in the sentence. Other Options 1. げんちょ – Not a standard reading of 顕著. 2. けんしょ – Does not match the word 顕著. 4. げんしょ – Not a standard reading of 顕著 and does not fit here. Only けんちょ corresponds correctly to the kanji 顕著 in this context.

questions.stem: 相談の内容は多岐にわたった。
opts: たき
かすれて
つぶれて
すたれて
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: たき is correct. Question Translation "相談の内容は多岐にわたった。" → "The contents of the consultation covered many topics." Explanation The kanji "多岐" is read as たき, which means *various* or *diverse*. This matches the context indicating that the consultation included many different subjects. Other Options 2. かすれて – Means *faded* or *blurred* (kanji: 掠れて), and does not fit the meaning of 多岐. 3. つぶれて – Means *collapsed* or *crushed* (kanji: 潰れて), irrelevant here. 4. すたれて – Means *obsolete* or *out of use* (kanji: 廃れて), not related to 多岐. Only たき matches the kanji 多岐 in this context.

questions.stem: その風習は、今はもう廃れてしまった。
opts: くずれて
かすれて
つぶれて
すたれて
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: すたれて is correct. Question Translation "その風習は、今はもう廃れてしまった。" → "That custom has now become obsolete." Explanation The kanji "廃れて" is read as すたれて, which means *to become obsolete* or *to go out of use*. This reading fits perfectly with the context, indicating that the custom is no longer practiced. Other Options 1. くずれて – Means *collapsed* or *crumbled* (kanji: 崩れて), not appropriate for 廃れて. 2. かすれて – Means *blurred* or *faint* (kanji: 掠れて), and does not match 廃れて. 3. つぶれて – Means *crushed* or *smashed* (kanji: 潰れて), unrelated to 廃れて. Only すたれて correctly matches the kanji 廃れて in this context.

questions.stem: 家賃の相場は地域によって違う。
opts: あいば
そうば
あいじょう
そうじょう
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: そうば is correct. Question Translation "家賃の相場は地域によって違う。" → "The market rate for rent differs by region." Explanation The kanji "相場" is read as そうば, which means *market rate* or *going rate*. This is the correct reading based on the kanji in the sentence. Other Options 1. あいば – This reading does not relate to any common meaning of 相場. 3. あいじょう – Means *affection* (kanji: 愛情), which has a different meaning and kanji. 4. そうじょう – Suggests *mutual, concurrency*, but does not fit here or match 相場. Only そうば is the appropriate reading for the kanji 相場 in this sentence.

questions.stem: 私はこの土地で定職に就き、生活の（ ）を築いた。
opts: 根拠
基盤
根源
基地
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 基盤 (きばん) is correct. It means "foundation" or "basis." Question Translation I secured a steady job in this area and established the foundation of my life. Explanation In this context, "foundation" refers to the stable and essential base one needs for living or for building up one's life. The phrase "生活の基盤" (せいかつのきばん) specifically means setting a stable base or foundation for life, which fits the sentence contextually since it talks about securing a job and thereby establishing life stability. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 根拠 (こんきょ) means "basis" or "grounds," often used to support claims or arguments. It doesn't fit in this context of establishing life stability. - Answer 3: 根源 (こんげん) means "origin" or "source," typically referring to the cause or origin of phenomena, not suitable for describing life stability. - Answer 4: 基地 (きち) means "base" or "military base," and is often used in the context of physical locations rather than abstract concepts like life foundation. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The term "基盤" (きばん) is often used in contexts that require stability and support, similar to laying down a foundation stone for building something substantial. Recognizing suitable contexts for specific words is crucial in Japanese, especially for expressions related to life and personal development.

questions.stem: 議論は難航すると思ったが、すぐに意見がまとまり、（ ）結論が出た。
opts: すんなり
うっとり
ふんわり
こっそり
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: すんなり is correct. It means "smoothly" or "without difficulty." Question Translation I thought the discussion would proceed with difficulty, but opinions came together quickly and a conclusion was reached smoothly. Explanation The sentence implies that despite an initial expectation of difficulty in the discussion, the process of reaching a conclusion was unexpectedly easy. The word すんなり fits this context as it means achieving something without much trouble, aligning with the surprise that opinions quickly converged and a conclusion was reached. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: うっとり means "spellbound" or "entranced." This has a completely irrelevant meaning to the context of reaching a conclusion in a discussion. - Answer 3: ふんわり means "softly" or "gently." While it can describe a gentle process, it doesn't fit the context of reaching a decision easily or smoothly. - Answer 4: こっそり means "secretly" or "stealthily." This implies doing something discreetly or in hiding, which does not match the context of an open discussion where opinions come together and a conclusion is reached. Grammar/Vocabulary Note すんなり is a common adverb used to describe actions or processes that happen easily or without resistance. It's often used in contexts where a task or decision flows naturally and without obstacles, making it a useful word for expressing smooth transitions or outcomes.

questions.stem: さっき駅前で佐藤さとうさんを（ ）んですが、今、海外にいるはずなのに変ですね。
opts: 見合わせた
見過ごした
見かけた
見違えた
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 見かけた is correct. It means "spotted" or "happened to see." Question Translation Earlier, I spotted Mr. Sato in front of the station, but it's strange because he is supposed to be overseas now. Explanation The sentence describes a situation where someone sees Mr. Sato in an unexpected place. The verb 見かける (みかける) means "to see unexpectedly" or "to spot," which fits perfectly because it implies a casual or unexpected sighting, matching the context of the sentence that expresses surprise about seeing Mr. Sato. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 見合わせた (みあわせた) means "to look at each other" or "postpone." It doesn’t fit the context of spotting or unexpectedly seeing someone. - Answer 2: 見過ごした (みすごした) means "to overlook" or "to fail to notice." It implies missing something, which is the opposite of spotting someone. - Answer 4: 見違えた (みちがえた) means "to mistake for someone else" or "to not recognize." It suggests recognizing incorrectly, which does not align with the context of having recognized someone you were surprised to see. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The verb 見かける is often used for describing an unexpected or casual sighting, emphasizing the surprise or randomness of the encounter. Recognizing the nuances between similar verbs involving "seeing" or "looking" is crucial for understanding contextually defined expressions in Japanese.

questions.stem: 市長の責任ある行動が住民の不安を（ ）し、行政に対する期待が一気に高まった。
opts: 一掃
追放
削除
排出
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: 一掃 (いっそう) is correct. It means "to sweep away" or "to eliminate completely." Question Translation The mayor's responsible actions swept away the residents' anxieties, and expectations of the administration rose dramatically. Explanation The context of the sentence is about the effects of the mayor's actions on the residents' feelings. The phrase "住民の不安を（ ）し" implies that the residents' anxieties were removed or eliminated. The term 一掃 means "to sweep away" or "to eliminate completely," which fits perfectly as it describes removing the anxieties entirely. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: 追放 (ついほう) means "banishment" or "expulsion." This term is used for expelling or exiling individuals or things from a place, not suitable for describing the removal of intangible feelings like anxiety. - Answer 3: 削除 (さくじょ) means "deletion" or "removal." While it does mean removal, it is typically used in contexts like deleting data or text, not suitable for abstract concepts like anxiety in this context. - Answer 4: 排出 (はいしゅつ) means "discharge" or "emission," usually referring to the release of substances like gas or liquids. It doesn't apply to the context of feelings or emotions. Grammar/Vocabulary Note Understanding compound words and their appropriate contexts is crucial. 一掃 (いっそう) is often used to describe the complete removal or eradication of negative elements, making it ideal for expressions involving elimination of anxieties or problems. Recognizing

questions.stem: 十分に煮た野菜は味が（ ）柔らかく、とてもおいしかった。
opts: 溶とけて
染そみて
潤うるおって
沈しずんで
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 染みて (そみて) is correct. It means "to soak in" or "to penetrate." Question Translation The vegetables that were boiled long enough were soaked with flavor, making them very tender and delicious. Explanation The sentence describes boiled vegetables that absorbed flavor during cooking. The correct word, 染みて, suggests that the flavor has thoroughly penetrated the vegetables, aligning with the context of describing both taste and texture intimately filled with flavor. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 溶けて (とけて) means "to melt" or "to dissolve." This implies a change in state rather than the absorption of flavor, and is therefore not suitable for describing vegetables in this cooking context. - Answer 3: 潤って (うるおって) means "to be moist" or "to hydrate." While unrelated to flavor absorption, it doesn’t specifically relate to taste or penetration of flavor, making it less suitable. - Answer 4: 沈んで (しずんで) means "to sink." This word doesn't correlate with how vegetables absorb flavor, and it relates more to movement or position in relation to gravity, not flavor or texture. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The verb 染みる (染みて being the te-form) is used to describe how a liquid or flavor penetrates into something. It captures the essence of flavor infusion, which is key in culinary contexts to describe how thoroughly seasonings permeate food items. Understanding these types of contextually defined expressions is crucial to correctly infer meaning in Japanese cooking

questions.stem: このテーブルは私が子どものころから使っているので、（ ）があって捨てられ ない。
opts: 心情
好感
熱意
愛着
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 愛着 (あいちゃく) is correct. It means "attachment" or "affection." Question Translation This table has been used by me since I was a child, so I can't throw it away because of my attachment to it. Explanation The sentence indicates a sentiment towards an object (a table) that has been part of the speaker's life since childhood. The word 愛着 (あいちゃく) perfectly captures the feeling of attachment or emotional connection one might develop towards an object over time, making it difficult to discard. This context of personal sentiment and nostalgia is ideally expressed by 愛着. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 心情 (しんじょう) means "feelings" or "sentiments." While it relates to emotions, it is more general and does not specifically convey the sense of attachment or connection to an object as 愛着 does. - Answer 2: 好感 (こうかん) means "good impression" or "favorable feeling." This term is often used to describe feelings towards people or experiences that are positive, but it doesn't convey a deep sense of attachment or inability to part with an object. - Answer 3: 熱意 (ねつい) means "enthusiasm" or "zeal." This suggests a passionate interest or drive, which doesn't fit the context of having a sentimental attachment to an object. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 愛着 (あいちゃく) is a compound word commonly used to describe an emotional bond or a sentimental attachment to objects, places, or sometimes people. Understanding such compound expressions and their specific contexts is crucial

questions.stem: 現社長は創立者から経営の（ ）を学んだ。
opts: データベース
ベテラン
ライフワーク
ノウハウ
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: ノウハウ is correct. It means "know-how," referring to practical knowledge or expertise in a specific area. Question Translation The current president learned the management know-how from the founder. Explanation The sentence discusses what the current president learned from the founder, specifically regarding management skills or expertise. The term ノウハウ (know-how) fits perfectly here as it refers to the practical knowledge or expertise necessary to effectively manage a company. In this context, it implies that the president gained the essential knowledge and skills for running the business from the founder. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: データベース, which means "database," doesn't fit contextually as it's not something one would typically learn directly from a founder in the context of management skills. - Answer 2: ベテラン (veteran) implies a person with experience, but it doesn’t refer to the knowledge or skills learned. It is irrelevant because it describes a person, not the knowledge acquired by the president. - Answer 3: ライフワーク (life-work) typically refers to one’s lifelong passion or major work, not relevant in this context, which focuses on practical management skills instead. Grammar/Vocabulary Note ノウハウ is derived from the English term "know-how" and is commonly used in business contexts to indicate practical skills or knowledge crucial for managing tasks or responsibilities. Recognizing borrowed terms and understanding their usage in nat

questions.stem: 高橋たかはしさんにはかねがね お会いしたいと思っていました。
opts: 直接
ぜひ
早く
以前から
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 以前から is correct. It means "since a while ago" or "for some time." Question Translation I have wanted to meet Mr. Takahashi for quite some time. Explanation The sentence expresses a desire that has persisted over a period of time. The phrase かねがね (kanegane) indicates something that has been continuously or frequently the case. The correct paraphrase should reflect this ongoing desire or intention. 以前から (いぜんから) adequately captures the sense of "since a while ago" or "for some time," aligning with かねがね. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 直接 (ちょくせつ) means "directly." It does not fit the context of time or duration. - Answer 2: ぜひ (zehi) means "certainly" or "by all means," which expresses a strong desire but does not convey the ongoing nature implied by かねがね. - Answer 3: 早く (はやく) means "quickly" or "soon," indicating urgency or immediacy rather than a continuing state over time. Grammar/Vocabulary Note かねがね is often used in formal contexts to express a state or feeling that has existed or been repeated over a period. It pairs naturally with terms indicating duration or time continuity, such as 以前から, to convey this sense in Japanese language expressions. It is essential to note the subtle nuances in temporal expressions when determining correct paraphrases in Japanese.

questions.stem: 林はやしさんはそれを故意に捨てたらしい。
opts: わざと
うっかり
いやいや
さっさと
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: わざと is correct. It means "intentionally" or "on purpose." Question Translation It seems that Mr./Ms. Hayashi intentionally threw it away. Explanation The sentence contains the word 故意 (こい), which means "deliberately" or "intentionally." The best paraphrase for "故意に捨てたらしい" is わざと, as it conveys the same meaning of doing something on purpose or with intention. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: うっかり means "carelessly" or "by mistake." This is the opposite of 故意, which implies intention rather than an accident. - Answer 3: いやいや means "reluctantly" or "unwillingly." This word doesn’t fit because it indicates a lack of willingness, not intention. - Answer 4: さっさと means "quickly" or "promptly." This describes speed, not the intent behind an action, and therefore doesn't match with the meaning of 故意. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The word 故意 (こい) is often used in legal or formal contexts to describe actions taken deliberately or with intent. Recognizing its paraphrase as わざと, a more colloquial term, is important for understanding nuances in spoken language.

questions.stem: 昨日、鈴木すず きさんにおわびした。
opts: 文句を言った
お礼を言った
断った
謝った
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 謝った is correct. It means "apologized." Question Translation Yesterday, I apologized to Mr. Suzuki. Explanation The sentence describes an action directed towards Mr. Suzuki, specifically indicating a form of communication or interaction. The word "おわびした" in the question suggests an action involving apology or expressing regret, which "謝った" (あやまった) accurately conveys as it means "apologized." Therefore, this answer fits the context perfectly. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 文句を言った (もんくをいった) means "complained." This contradicts the context of an apologetic action, as complaining is expressing dissatisfaction rather than seeking forgiveness. - Answer 2: お礼を言った (おれいをいった) means "thanked." While polite, it doesn’t convey the sense of apology or expressing regret, as it focuses on gratitude instead. - Answer 3: 断った (ことわった) means "refused" or "declined." This doesn’t align with the context of apology and instead implies rejecting something, which is unrelated to the concept of apologizing. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 謝る (あやまる) is a verb that means "to apologize." It’s important to distinguish words in Japanese that might involve expressions of emotion or interactions, as their nuances can significantly change the meaning of the sentence. Recognizing these differences is crucial for selecting the correct paraphrase that maintains the original intent of the given sentence.

questions.stem: 中村なかむらさんの言葉からは強い意気込みが伝わってくる。
opts: 敬意
自信
意欲
信頼
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 意欲 (いよく) is correct. It means "eagerness" or "enthusiasm." Question Translation From Mr. Nakamura's words, you can sense a strong eagerness. Explanation The sentence discusses the impression conveyed through Mr. Nakamura's words. 意欲 (いよく) refers to a strong desire or eagerness, which aligns with the idea of someone expressing determined enthusiasm or motivation. This fits the context of perceiving strong feelings or determination in someone's words. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 敬意 (けいい) means "respect." It does not fit because the sentence focuses on a strong emotion or attitude originating from Nakamura, rather than a feeling of respect from the speaker toward Nakamura. - Answer 2: 自信 (じしん) means "confidence." While confidence might accompany enthusiasm, the sentence specifically conveys a sense of eagerness rather than purely self-assurance. - Answer 4: 信頼 (しんらい) means "trust." This is unrelated to the context, as the sentence is about Mr. Nakamura's expressed eagerness or intent, not about trust being conveyed. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 意欲 (いよく) is crucial here as it helps convey the sentiment of being driven and ready for action. Understanding the nuance between similar feelings like eagerness (意欲) and confidence (自信) can be key in identifying the most context-appropriate term in paraphrase questions.

questions.stem: 妹は少しおびえているようだった。
opts: 焦って
怖がって
悩んで
悔やんで
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 怖がって (こわがって) is correct. It means "afraid" or "scared." Question Translation It seemed like my younger sister was a little scared. Explanation The verb phrase "おびえている" means "being frightened" or "showing fear." The sentence indicates the emotional state of the speaker's younger sister, and 怖がって (こわがって) is a direct synonym, meaning "being afraid" or "scared." It matches the tone and emotional state implied in the sentence. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 焦って (あせって) means "being in a hurry" or "anxious" due to impatience. It implies a sense of urgency or impatience, which doesn't match the emotional state of being scared. - Answer 3: 悩んで (なやんで) means "worrying" or "troubled." It indicates being preoccupied with a problem or concern, not the sense of fear implied by "おびえている." - Answer 4: 悔やんで (くやんで) means "regretting." This is associated with feeling sorry or regretful about past actions and doesn’t match the fear or fright suggested by the original sentence. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The verb "おびえる" (怯える) is often used to describe a state of fear or fright, typically seen in contexts where someone is visibly scared. Recognizing synonyms like 怖がって is crucial for accurately determining the emotional tone of statements in Japanese.

questions.stem: 私はその一言に安堵あん どした。
opts: すっとした
はっとした
ほっとした
かっとした
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: ほっとした is correct. It means "felt relieved" or "felt at ease." Question Translation The sentence translates to: "I felt relieved by those few words." Explanation The expression "ほっとする" conveys a sense of relief, which perfectly matches the emotion of feeling reassured or relaxed after hearing certain comforting or decisive words. In the context of the sentence, "その一言" suggests that a single phrase or word provided a sense of calm or relief to the speaker, which aligns with the use of "ほっとした." Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: すっとした implies a feeling of refreshment or a situation where something becomes smooth or clear, which does not match the context of feeling relieved. - Answer 2: はっとした means "startled" or "surprised." It connotes a sudden reaction, which contrasts with the comfort or relief implied in the question. - Answer 4: かっとした describes a sudden burst of anger or irritation, which also does not fit the context of feeling relieved after hearing certain words. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The verb "ほっとする" is commonly used to describe the relief felt after a stressful situation resolves or when hearing reassuring news. Understanding these subtle expression variations in emotional contexts is crucial for interpreting Japanese language nuances accurately.

questions.stem: 閑静
opts: そのレストランは繁華街から外れた閑静な場所にある。
今日は朝から具合が悪かったので、会社を休んで家で閑静にしていた。
用事が早く済み、閑静な時間ができたので、映画を見に行くことにした。
日中はにぎやかな公園だが、夜になると急に閑静になる。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1 is correct. Word Translation 閑静（かんせい） — quiet, peaceful, tranquil Correct Sentence Translation The restaurant is located in a quiet place away from the busy downtown area. Explanation The word 閑静 refers to a state of being quiet and peaceful, typically used to describe locations like neighborhoods or areas. "閑静な場所" (a quiet place) is a natural expression suggesting an area that is tranquil and not bustling with activity, making it suitable for residential or dining areas. Other Options ② 今日は朝から具合が悪かったので、会社を休んで家で__閑静__にしていた。 → I felt unwell since morning, so I took a day off work and quietly stayed at home. Awkward Usage While "quietly stayed" might seem fitting, "閑静" is specifically an adjective for places or surroundings, not actions or states of being. A better word here would be "静かに" or "安静に". ③ 用事が早く済み、__閑静__な時間ができたので、映画を見に行くことにした。 → I finished my tasks early and had some quiet time, so I decided to go see a movie. Awkward Usage "閑静" is not used to describe time directly; it describes spatial atmospheres. To indicate a peaceful period of time, "静かな時間" or "ゆっくりできる時間" would be more appropriate. ④ 日中はにぎやかな公園だが、夜になると急に__閑静__になる。 → The park is lively during the day, but it becomes suddenly quiet at night. Awkward Usage Though the concept of a place becoming quiet might seem related, "閑静" generally refers to a naturally tranquil place, not just a temporary or situational quiet like at night. "静かになる" would be more suitable for describing such a change. U

questions.stem: たやすい
opts: 弟は寝坊したらしく、たやすい物だけ食べて、慌てて出かけていった。
伊藤いとう氏とは大学時代からの親友で、本音ほんねが言えるたやすい関係だ。
せっかくの日曜日だから、ゆっくり休んでたやすく過ごそうと思う。
この問題は想像以上に複雑で、たやすく解決できるものではなかった。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4 is correct. Word Translation たやすい — easy, simple Correct Sentence Translation This problem is more complex than I imagined and wasn't something that could be solved easily. Explanation The word たやすい is used to describe tasks or problems that can be done without much difficulty. In this context, たやすく解決できる (can be solved easily) accurately captures the meaning of taking little effort to solve a problem, so its usage here is natural. Other Options ① 弟は寝坊したらしく、__たやすい__物だけ食べて、慌てて出かけていった。 → My little brother seems to have overslept and quickly ate only some easy food before rushing out. Awkward Usage While "たやすい物" might seem to mean "easy food," it is not a common or natural phrase in Japanese. A more appropriate word would be "簡単な" (simple) when discussing the ease of preparing or eating food. ② [伊藤|いとう]氏とは大学時代からの親友で、[本音|ほんね]が言える__たやすい__関係だ。 → Mr. Ito is a close friend from my university days, and we have an easy relationship where we can speak honestly. Awkward Usage Though "たやすい" means easy, it is typically not used to describe relationships. More natural expressions would use "気楽な" (comfortable) or "気軽に話せる" (can talk freely). ③ せっかくの日曜日だから、ゆっくり休んで__たやすく__過ごそうと思う。 → Since it’s a precious Sunday, I plan to relax and spend it easily. Awkward Usage The phrase "たやすく過ごす" is not idiomatic in Japanese. A more natural phrase might be "のんびり過ごす" (spend leisurely) or "ゆっくり休む" (relax leisurely). Usage Note "たやすい" is best applied to situations or tasks where the proce

questions.stem: 察する
opts: 医師たちはチームを組み、意見を出し合って、最良の治療法を察した。
気象予報士はテレビの天気予報で、来週の気温の変化を察し始めた。
鈴木すずきさんは、私が何も言わなくても、私の気持ちを察して慰めてくれた。
外を歩いていたら急にいいアイディアを察したので、手帳にメモをした。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3 is correct. Word Translation 察する（さっする） — to guess, to sense, to infer Correct Sentence Translation Mr. Suzuki comforted me by sensing my feelings without me saying anything. Explanation The word 察する is used to describe the act of intuitively understanding something without explicit communication. It is often employed in contexts where one senses someone else's emotions or thoughts. "私の気持ちを察して" is a common and natural use of this word, as it concerns understanding an unspoken feeling. Other Options ① 医師たちはチームを組み、意見を出し合って、最良の治療法を__察した__。 → The doctors formed a team, exchanged opinions, and inferred the best treatment. Awkward Usage "察する" implies intuitive guessing. For analyzing structured suggestions, a term like "決定した" (decided) or "考案した" (devised) is more fitting. ② 気象予報士はテレビの天気予報で、来週の気温の変化を__察し__始めた。 → The meteorologist started inferring next week’s temperature changes on the TV forecast. Incorrect Usage "察する" is inappropriate for systematic prediction tasks like weather forecasts. Instead, use "予測し始めた" (began to predict). ④ 外を歩いていたら急にいいアイディアを__察した__ので、手帳にメモをした。 → While walking outside, I suddenly guessed a good idea, so I jotted it down in my notebook. Incorrect Usage "察する" is not used for coming up with ideas. A more suitable verb would be "思いついた" (came up with). Usage Note "察する" is best suited for contexts involving intuition or empathy, especially in interpersonal understanding. It’s not typically used for formal decision-making, technical predic

questions.stem: 内訳
opts: 来週の内訳を確認したが、予定がないのは木曜の夜だけだ。
前回の出張費の内訳を見たら、交通費の割合が予想外に高かった。
司会者は進行を間違えないように、式の内訳を何度も見直した。
家族の健康のため、栄養の内訳を考えて食事を作っている。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2 is correct. Word Translation 内訳（うちわけ） — breakdown, itemization Correct Sentence Translation When I looked at the breakdown of the last business trip expenses, the proportion of transportation costs was unexpectedly high. Explanation The word 内訳 refers to breaking something down into its component parts, often used for financial or numerical details. It fits naturally with expense-related contexts like "出張費の内訳" (breakdown of travel expenses), making it the correct choice in this sentence. Other Options ① 来週の__内訳__を確認したが、予定がないのは木曜の夜だけだ。 → I checked the breakdown for next week but only have free time on Thursday night. Awkward Usage "内訳" would be unnatural because "breakdown" usually applies to detailed lists or costs, not schedules. A more fitting word would be "予定表" (schedule). ③ 司会者は進行を間違えないように、式の__内訳__を何度も見直した。 → The host reviewed the breakdown of the ceremony many times to avoid making mistakes. Awkward Usage While 内訳 could imply details of the event, "進行表" or "プログラム" (program) would better describe a schedule or order of events. ④ 家族の健康のため、栄養の__内訳__を考えて食事を作っている。 → For the family's health, I prepare meals considering the nutritional breakdown. Awkward Usage Although this structure appears logical, 内訳 is usually used for numerical or financial data. "バランス" (balance) or "栄養価" (nutritional value) would be more naturally used in this context. Usage Note "内訳" is primarily used in contexts involving numerical data or costs. When describing detailed content

questions.stem: 食い違う
opts: この事件は、複数の目撃者の話がそれぞれ食い違っており、不明な点が多い。
金庫を開けようと思ったが、どの鍵も食い違って開けられなかった。
何だか歩きにくいと思ったら、サンダルの左右が食い違っていた。
調味料を変えたのか、この料理はいつもと味が食い違っているように感じる。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1 is correct. Word Translation 食い違う（くいちがう） — to be in disagreement or conflict, to be inconsistent Correct Sentence Translation In this case, the accounts of multiple witnesses are conflicting with each other, leaving many unclear points. Explanation The word 食い違う is used to describe situations where opinions, statements, or information do not match, leading to inconsistencies or disagreements. It naturally fits contexts involving different versions or contradictions, as seen with witness accounts in a legal or investigative scenario. Other Options ② 金庫を開けようと思ったが、どの鍵も__食い違って__開けられなかった。 → I wanted to open the safe, but none of the keys fit, so I couldn't open it. Incorrect Usage "食い違う" is not suitable here; it doesn't describe a physical mismatch like keys and locks. The phrase "合わない" (don’t fit) is more appropriate. ③ 何だか歩きにくいと思ったら、サンダルの左右が__食い違って__いた。 → I felt it was hard to walk and realized that the left and right sandals were mismatched. Incorrect Usage "食い違う" is not used for physical mismatches like footwear. "間違った" (mismatched) would be a better fit. ④ 調味料を変えたのか、この料理はいつもと味が__食い違って__いるように感じる。 → I feel this dish tastes different from usual; maybe the seasonings were changed. Incorrect Usage "食い違う" does not apply to taste differences. Use terms like "変わっている" (has changed) instead. Usage Note "食い違う" is generally reserved for abstract or conceptual conflicts, such as opinions, statements, or reports that do not align. It should not be used for physical

questions.stem: 過密
opts: 雑誌で紹介されてから、この商品への過密な注文が続いているらしい。
水質汚染に関して人々の抗議が過密になり、政府は対策を迫られている。
今回の出張は過密なスケジュールで、ゆっくり食事する時間もなさそうだ。
春になると、この池の周りには、色とりどりの花が過密に咲き乱れる。
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3 is correct. Word Translation 過密（かみつ） — overcrowded, tightly packed, dense Correct Sentence Translation This business trip has such a hectic schedule, it seems there won’t even be time for a leisurely meal. Explanation The word 過密 is used to describe situations that are very busy or tightly packed. "過密なスケジュール" (a hectic schedule) is a common and natural phrase in Japanese, indicating a schedule that is tightly packed with activities. Other Options ① 雑誌で紹介されてから、この商品への__過密__な注文が続いているらしい。 → After being featured in a magazine, it seems there are continuously dense orders for this product. Incorrect Usage "過密" does not fit when describing orders. Words like "多くの" or "殺到する" would be more appropriate. ② 水質汚染に関して人々の抗議が__過密__になり、政府は対策を迫られている。 → Regarding water pollution, the protests of people have become dense, and the government is urged to take action. Awkward Usage While "過密" could imply a large number, it doesn't describe the nature of protests properly. More suitable expressions include "激化する" or "増加する". ④ 春になると、この池の周りには、色とりどりの花が__過密__に咲き乱れる。 → In spring, colorful flowers bloom densely around this pond. Incorrect Usage "過密" is generally for schedules or data, not for natural phenomena like blooming flowers. The word "たくさん" or "咲きこぼれる" suits better. Usage Note "過密" is commonly associated with schedules, events, or situations dealing with time, space, or data being tightly packed. It is not suitable for natural or emotional descriptions.

questions.stem: 朝の満員電車。車内の混雑を（ ） 、私の目の前に座っている学生風の男は、平然とノートパソコンを広げて、作業に没頭していた。
opts: 含めて
もとに
除いて
よそに
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: よそに is correct. It means "ignoring" or "without regard to." Question Translation During the crowded morning train, the young man, who looked like a student and was sitting in front of me, opened his laptop and became absorbed in his work, ignoring the congestion inside the train. Explanation The correct choice here is よそに, which is used in expressions that denote ignoring or acting without regard to surrounding conditions or situations. In this context, the sentence describes how the man was unfazed by the crowded conditions of the train and went about his work undisturbed. よそに helps convey this sense of ignoring the external circumstances. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 含めて (ふくめて) means "including." This doesn't fit the context as it would imply that the man's actions included being aware of the crowdedness, which is opposite of what is meant. - Answer 2: もとに means "based on" or "under." This phrase typically indicates a source or basis for something, which doesn't make sense for expressing the idea of ignoring the crowded conditions. - Answer 3: 除いて (のぞいて) means "excluding" or "aside from." It doesn’t align with the intended meaning of ignoring; instead, it would suggest separating or removing something from consideration, not disregarding it. Grammar/Vocabulary Note よそに is often used in the pattern "～をよそに," indicating the act of ignoring or being indifferent to the surrounding situation or condition. It's a useful phrase in conveying t

questions.stem: 私の父は、（ ）もしないで漫画を批判するから、本当に嫌になる。
opts: 読まない
読み
読もう
読んで
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: 読み is correct. It means "to read," forming the negative form "読まない" as in "without reading." Question Translation My father criticizes manga without even reading them, so it really annoys me. Explanation The sentence structure is looking for a form that connects with "もしないで," which means "without doing (something)." The verb stem "読み" from "読む" fits here because it combines with "もしないで" to indicate the action that is not being done. The construction "読みもしないで" emphasizes the father's lack of action — he criticizes manga without even bothering to read them. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 読まない is the negative form of "to read," but it cannot directly pair with "しないで" to properly convey "without even doing." - Answer 3: 読もう is the volitional form, meaning "let's read" or "I shall read," which does not fit with "しないで," as it changes the intended meaning to something like "without intending to read." - Answer 4: 読んで is the te-form of "to read," which is used for sequences of events or requests, and it doesn’t fit in the context of "without even doing." Grammar/Vocabulary Note The phrase "(verb stem)もしないで" stresses the absence of an expected action. It is often used to express frustration or disbelief that someone criticizes or makes decisions about something they haven't even experienced themselves. Recognizing verb conjugation and form usage is crucial for understanding these nuances.

questions.stem: （卒業生へのインタビューで）聞き手「学生時代にやったことで、今の仕事に役立っていることは何でしょうか。」田中たなか「ラグビー部での経験ですね。チームワークの大切さを痛感しました。（ ）、それは去年企画チームのリーダーになって初めて気づいたことですが。」
opts: 要するに
あるいは
もっとも
ついては
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: もっとも is correct. It means "however" or "though." Question Translation (Interview with a graduate) Interviewer: "What did you do during your student days that is helpful in your current job?" Tanaka: "It's the experience from the rugby club. I keenly realized the importance of teamwork. (However), that was something I only realized for the first time when I became the leader of the planning team last year." Explanation The sentence is discussing an experience that was pivotal in developing teamwork skills. The word もっとも introduces contrast or an additional comment—specifically that the speaker truly realized the importance when they became a team leader. This nuance of adding an afterthought or condition makes もっとも apt for the context. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 要するに (ようするに) means "in other words" or "to summarize." This doesn't fit as it doesn't introduce a contrast or an afterthought but rather a summary or conclusion. - Answer 2: あるいは means "or" or "possibly." It indicates an alternative or choice, which is not relevant in this context since the speaker is clarifying the timing of their realization, not offering options. - Answer 4: ついては means "therefore" or "with regard to," suggesting a logical consequence or addressal of a topic, not introducing an additional realization or condition. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The use of もっとも to insert an afterthought or nuance is common in Japanese, where a concessive tone is needed to communicate

questions.stem: 村の郷土史をまとめるにあたり、今年90歳になる元村長の東山ひがしやまさんにお話を伺った。 村に初めて汽車が走ったときのことを鮮明に覚えて（ ）、その記憶力に驚いた。
opts: おいでになり
差し上げ
まいり
申し上げ

questions.stem: （求人サイトの「よくある質問」で）Ｑ：インテリアに関する知識がないのですが、働けますか。Ａ：研修があるので大丈夫です。知識はある（ ）が、それよりも人柄や仕事 に取り組む姿勢を重視しています。
opts: にすぎません
ことは否めません
に越したことはありません
といっても過言ではありません
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: に越したことはありません is correct. It means "it's better (to have)". Question Translation Q: I don't have any knowledge about interiors, but can I still work? A: There is a training program, so it's okay. It's better to have knowledge, but we value personality and attitude towards work more. Explanation The phrase に越したことはありません (にこしたことはありません) is used to express the idea that while something isn't necessary, having it would be preferable or better. In this context, the answer implies that having knowledge is advantageous but not crucial, as other qualities are considered more important. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: にすぎません means "no more than" or "just". It implies limitation or something trivial, which doesn’t fit the context of expressing a preferable condition. - Answer 2: ことは否めません (ことはいなめません) means "it cannot be denied", expressing an undeniable fact. This doesn’t convey an opinion about preference. - Answer 4: といっても過言ではありません (といってもかごんではありません) means "it wouldn't be an exaggeration to say". This suggests a strong assertion, not suitability or optional preference as needed here. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The expression に越したことはありません is often used to suggest that, while something is not required, it would certainly be beneficial. This structure highlights preferences and advantages in a nuanced manner, indicating that while not essential, having that particular attribute is certainly desirable. Understanding such expressions is essential for grasping

questions.stem: （お知らせで） 水道管破裂による断水のため、８月１ ２日まで市民プールの営業を休止します。復旧 状況（ ） 、営業再開が遅れる可能性がありますので、ご了承ください。
opts: 次第には
次第に
次第では
次第
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 3: 次第では is correct. It means "depending on (the situation)." Question Translation Due to a water pipe burst, the municipal pool's operations will be suspended until August 12. Depending on the recovery situation, there is a possibility that the reopening will be delayed, so please understand. Explanation 次第では is used when indicating that some outcome is contingent upon certain conditions. In this context, "復旧状況次第では" means "depending on the condition of the recovery situation," which directly affects whether the pool reopening will be delayed. This structure highlights the conditional nature typical of operational or planning contexts, framed around specific circumstances. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 次第には is incorrect because 次第には is not a grammatically correct phrase. The particle に can be used with 次第 to imply an order of events or a decision, but combined with は, it doesn't fit in this context. - Answer 2: 次第に means "gradually" or "progressively," which doesn't relate to this sentence's context of describing conditions impacting the reopening of the pool. - Answer 4: 次第 alone means "depending on" or "as soon as" when used differently, but requires a change in sentence structure to convey the potential conditionality expressed in this sentence. Grammar/Vocabulary Note 次第 (しだい) can shift in meaning based on its particle companion or context: - When used with では, it implies conditionality, as seen in this problem. - When used with particle

questions.stem: クレジットカードの番号等、他人に（ ）困る情報は、電子メールには書かな いほうがいいそうだ。
opts: 知っていても
知っていなくても
知らなくては
知られては
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 知られては is correct. It means "if it is known" or "if it becomes known." Question Translation It's said that information, such as credit card numbers, which would be troublesome if known by others, should not be written in emails. Explanation The sentence is advising about maintaining privacy and is concerned with the potential negative consequences of certain information being known by others. 知られては uses the passive form with the conditional meaning "if it is known by others." This fits the context perfectly as the sentence talks about information that should be kept from becoming known to others to avoid problems. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 知っていても means "even if they know." This doesn't fit because the implication is not merely about accepting the consequence of others knowing, but rather about actively avoiding it. - Answer 2: 知っていなくても means "even if they don't know." This contradicts the caution being advised since the focus is on avoiding scenarios where information becomes known. - Answer 3: 知らなくては suggests an obligation, "must not be unknown." However, the goal here isn't about obliging someone to know, but rather to avoid the situation altogether. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The structure 知られては describes an undesirable or problematic scenario ("if it becomes known"), commonly used to hint at negative consequences and serves as a basis for recommendations or advisories, such as not including sensitive information in emails. Recognizin

questions.stem: 仕事は、決められた時間内に、いかに成果を上げるかが大切であり、単に時間をか けて（ ）と私は思う。
opts: がんばることだといってもおかしくない
がんばればいいというものではない
がんばることでしかない
がんばりようがない
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: がんばればいいというものではない is correct. It means "simply working hard is not all that matters." Question Translation In work, achieving results within the allotted time is important, and I think that simply spending time (and working hard) is not everything. Explanation The sentence emphasizes the importance of efficiency and results over merely the amount of time or effort spent. The phrase "がんばればいいというものではない" (ganbareba ii to iu mono dewa nai) indicates that just because you work hard, doesn't mean it's sufficient or correct, which aligns with the sentence's emphasis on productivity rather than just effort. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: がんばることだといってもおかしくない means "it's not strange to say 'working hard is it,'" which would contradict the emphasis on efficiency and is not logically consistent with the statement in the main sentence. - Answer 3: がんばることでしかない means "it's nothing but working hard," which incorrectly suggests that effort alone is paramount, misaligning with the sentence's focus on outcomes within a limited time. - Answer 4: がんばりようがない means "there is no way to try," which implies a lack of effort or impossibility, contrary to the sentence's theme of effective effort leading to results. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The phrase "というものではない" (to iu mono dewa nai) is often used to express that something is not necessarily or not exactly the way it is typically thought to be — challenging assumptions or common perceptions. In this context, it negates t

questions.stem: 現在、潜水調査船を用いた調査研究が進展中であり、いずれ近いうちに海底のより 詳細な地質構造が明らかに（ ） 。
opts: なるものと思われる
するという思いがある
なったかに思える
するだろうと思う
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 1: なるものと思われる is correct. It means "is expected to become." Question Translation Currently, research and surveys using underwater research vessels are progressing, and it is expected that the more detailed geological structure of the seabed will become clear in the near future. Explanation The phrase "なるものと思われる" is a formal and objective way to express an expectation or probability. In this context, it indicates an anticipation that a future development will clarify the geological structures beneath the sea. This expression is suitable here because the sentence projects a view of what is expected to happen as the research progresses, highlighting a probable outcome. Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 2: するという思いがある translates to "there is a thought of doing," which implies a subjective intention or plan, not suitable here because the sentence describes a research progression and outcome, not a personal thought or plan. - Answer 3: なったかに思える means "seems to have become," which suggests a conclusion about a present state rather than an anticipation of future clarity. - Answer 4: するだろうと思う translates to "I think it will probably do," which is more subjective and does not fit the formal and objective tone expected in scientific research discussing future developments. Grammar/Vocabulary Note - "なるものと思われる" is a formal expression often used in written Japanese to describe expectations or projections in a neutral or objective manner. It implies an evidence-ba

questions.stem: 山下やました 「あのう、西村にしむら先輩。私、今日でこのサークルを（ ）。」西村にしむら「ええ？ 辞める？ 急にどうして？」
opts: 辞めてしまわれたのでしょうか
辞めてしまったのかと思って
辞めさせたらどうでしょうか
辞めさせてもらおうかと思って
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 4: 辞めさせてもらおうかと思って is correct. It means "I am thinking of quitting." Question Translation Yamashita: "Um, Nishimura-senpai. I am thinking of quitting this club today." Nishimura: "Huh? Quit? Why so suddenly?" Explanation The question involves Yamashita expressing the intention to quit the club. The form 辞めさせてもらおうかと思って (やめさせてもらおうかとおもって) is used to indicate a speaker’s consideration of quitting. It implies that the speaker is considering asking for permission to leave, which fits the context of Yamashita tentatively broaching the subject with a senior (senpai). Why Other Options Are Incorrect - Answer 1: 辞めてしまわれたのでしょうか (やめてしまわれたのでしょうか) translates to "Have you quit?" implying someone else quitting, which doesn’t fit since Yamashita is talking about his own action. - Answer 2: 辞めてしまったのかと思って (やめてしまったのかとおもって) translates to "I thought you had quit," implying surprise or realization about someone else quitting, which doesn’t match the self-referential context. - Answer 3: 辞めさせたらどうでしょうか (やめさせたらどうでしょうか) means "How about you make them quit?" which implies suggesting someone else to quit, which is not suitable as Yamashita is discussing their own intention to quit. Grammar/Vocabulary Note The structure ～てもらおうかと思って (～てもらおうかとおもって) is used to express one's intention to take an action, often needing approval, in a tentative manner. In this case, 辞めさせてもらおう means “to be allowed to quit,” reflecting an intention with politeness towards the possible inconvenience to others.

questions.stem: 「アセビ」という、白い花を咲かせる樹木を漢字で「馬酔木」と書くのは、アセビ には ★ ______ ______ ______ そうです。
opts: 由来する
有毒成分があり
状態になることに
馬が食べると酔ったような
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 2: 有毒成分があり（★ is 1st: ★ ___ ___ ___） Correct Order 2 → 4 → 3 → 1 有毒成分があり → 馬が食べると酔ったような → 状態になることに → 由来する Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ② 有毒成分があり — “has toxic components”; あり links to the next clause (“and …”). ④ 馬が食べると酔ったような — “when a horse eats (it), (it’s) like being drunk”; modifies 状態. ③ 状態になることに — “the fact of becoming that state”; こと nominalises; に marks the source for 由来する. ① 由来する — “to derive/originate from”; concludes the explanation of the name’s origin. Reconstructed Sentence 「アセビ」という、白い花を咲かせる樹木を漢字で「馬酔木」と書くのは、アセビには有毒成分があり、馬が食べると酔ったような状態になることに由来するそうです。 → The reason the tree with white flowers called “asebi” is written in kanji as “馬酔木” is said to be that asebi contains toxic components, and when horses eat it, they become as if drunk. Why This Order Works - 有毒成分があり sets the cause and links forward. - 馬が食べると酔ったような modifies 状態 to form a natural noun phrase. - 状態になることに creates a nominalised source marked by に. - 由来する takes that nominal phrase as the origin of the name.

questions.stem: 家族の時間を大切にする夫は、つい ______ ______ ★ ______ ありがた い存在です。
opts: 本当に大切なものは何なのか
私に
仕事に夢中になりすぎる
気づかせてくれる
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 1: 本当に大切なものは何なのか Star position: 3rd (pattern is “___ ___ ★ ___”) Correct Order 3 → 2 → 1 → 4 仕事に夢中になりすぎる → 私に → 本当に大切なものは何なのか → 気づかせてくれる Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ③ 仕事に夢中になりすぎる — “get too absorbed in work”; relative clause modifying 私, scoped by つい (“inadvertently”). ② 私に — “to me/for me”; marks the target of the causative-benefactive. ① 本当に大切なものは何なのか — “what truly is important”; embedded question functioning as the object of 気づかせてくれる. ④ 気づかせてくれる — causative + てくれる (“makes/lets [me] realize [for my benefit]”). Reconstructed Sentence 家族の時間を大切にする夫は、つい仕事に夢中になりすぎる私に、本当に大切なものは何なのか気づかせてくれる、ありがたい存在です。 → My husband, who values family time, is such a blessing; when I end up getting too absorbed in work, he makes me realize what truly matters. Why This Order Works - つい naturally modifies 仕事に夢中になりすぎる. - 私に marks the person who is made to realize. - 本当に大切なものは何なのか is the content realized, taken by 気づかせてくれる. - Thus chunk 1 lands at the 3rd (★) position.

questions.stem: Z県知事の林はやし和夫かずお氏は、週刊誌で、脱税を行った______ ______★______ 異なり、名誉を傷つけられたとして、発行元のX社を相手取り訴訟を起こした。
opts: 事実とは全く
疑いがあるなどと
報じられた
ことに対し
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 4: ことに対し（★ is 3rd） Correct Order 2 → 3 → 4 → 1 疑いがあるなどと → 報じられた → ことに対し → 事実とは全く Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ② 疑いがあるなどと — “saying (things like) there is suspicion”; などと marks a loose/summary quotation before a reporting verb ③ 報じられた — passive “was reported”; completes the quoted content ④ ことに対し — “in response to the fact that …”; nominalises the preceding clause and adds the stance “regarding/against” ① 事実とは全く — “completely different from the facts”; とは sets the standard for comparison, pairs with 異なり Reconstructed Sentence Z県知事の林（はやし）和夫（かずお）氏は、週刊誌で、脱税を行った疑いがあるなどと報じられたことに対し、事実とは全く異なり、名誉を傷つけられたとして、発行元のX社を相手取り訴訟を起こした。 → Governor Hayashi Kazuo of Z Prefecture filed a lawsuit against publisher X company, asserting that, in response to reports in a weekly magazine saying he was suspected of having committed tax evasion, the reports were completely different from the facts and damaged his reputation. Why This Order Works - 疑いがあるなどと sets up the quoted allegation. - 報じられた is the reporting verb in passive. - ことに対し nominalises that reported fact and introduces his counter-stance (★ 3rd). - 事実とは全く directly precedes 異なり to express “completely different from the facts.”

questions.stem: Ｑ鉄道が１０年ぶりに運賃値上げに踏み切った。安全対策や原油の高騰で支出が増え、 経営努力だけでは対応しきれないと判断 ______ ______ ★ ______ 。
opts: という
の
ことだ
して
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 3: ことだ (3rd position) Correct Order 4 → 2 → 3 → 1 して → の → ことだ → という Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ④ して — te-form of する; links to 判断 to make 判断して “judging (that) …” ② の — nominaliser/connector; Vての + こと turns the action into a noun phrase “the act/result of having done …” ③ ことだ — “it is the case/that’s the reason”; in the set phrase 〜してのことだ = “it was because …” ① という — quotative/hearsay; sentence-final “it is said/reported that …” Reconstructed Sentence Ｑ鉄道が１０年ぶりに運賃値上げに踏み切った。安全対策や原油の高騰で支出が増え、経営努力だけでは対応しきれないと判断してのことだという。 → Q Railway raised fares for the first time in 10 years. Costs rose due to safety measures and soaring crude oil prices, and it is said that the move was because they judged it couldn’t be handled by management efforts alone. Why This Order Works - 判断 + して forms “judging (that) …” to connect the reason. - の nominalises the preceding clause to modify こと. - 〜してのことだ states “that’s the reason (for the action).” - という adds the reported/hearsay tone typical of news style.

questions.stem: 世の中にはさまざまな資格があふれているが、資格を取った ______ ______ ______ ★ 世間は甘くないらしい。
opts: だけ
ほど
で
希望の職につける
questions.expl: Answer (★ Position) Answer 2: ほど（★ is 4th: right before 「世間は甘くないらしい」） Correct Order 1 → 3 → 4 → 2 だけ → で → 希望の職につける → ほど Chunk-by-Chunk Breakdown ① だけ — “only/just”; pairs with で to make だけで “just by ~” ③ で — particle; with だけ forms だけで “simply by (doing/getting) ~” ④ 希望の職につける — “be able to land one’s desired job”; degree/extent clause before ほど ② ほど — “to the extent that”; with a negative, “not so ~ as to …” Reconstructed Sentence 世の中にはさまざまな資格があふれているが、資格を取っただけで希望の職につけるほど、世間は甘くないらしい。 → There are all sorts of qualifications out there, but it seems the world isn’t so easy that you can land your desired job just by getting a qualification. Why This Order Works - だけ + で forms the fixed expression “just by ~,” setting a minimal condition. - 希望の職につける provides the result being measured. - ほど marks the extent: “not (so lenient) as to be able to do X.” - Thus, “Society isn’t lenient to the extent that you can get your dream job just by getting a qualification.”

opts: こちらにはいえないこともない
こちらにいえるとは限らない
こちらにもいえるとは思わなかった
こちらにはいえないと思うのか
questions.expl: Correct Answer Answer 2: こちらにいえるとは限らない is correct. It means "it does not always apply here" or "it is not necessarily the case here." Question Translation There are manuals that exist in this world. When you buy a machine, you read it. If it doesn't operate as written, it's a problem. Pressing the play button on a video but starting a recording would be unbearable. However, living creatures are not like that. What applies there, [41]. Explanation This sentence discusses the contrast between the predictability of machines, which operate according to manuals, and the unpredictability of living creatures, which do not always behave consistently.
